old_claim.note: Port Cranes & Harbor Works provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because fortified water gates are wall and river-access defenses, while the corrected port_crane_harbor_works node is anchored to later stationary harbor-crane infrastructure.

source_shape.source_locator: Durham World Heritage Site describes masonry walls built around the peninsula between 1099 and 1128, strengthened with towers, and including gates leading to river fords such as Water Gate.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports fortified water gates as defensive wall and river-access features, not as a dependency of dockside harbor cranes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The water-gate source grounds the node in fortification infrastructure. Keeping a direct dependency on a 1244 harbor-crane node would create a later and unsupported prerequisite for an 1150 water-gate claim.

ontology_before: Fortified Water Gates depended on Port Cranes & Harbor Works, implying wall gates and river-access defenses required later stationary harbor-crane infrastructure.
ontology_after: Fortified Water Gates depends directly on Early Fortifications, with the later harbor-crane infrastructure kept separate.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the direct port_crane_harbor_works dependency is removed from fortified_water_gates.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to fortified crane-gates specifically rather than water gates in defensive walls.
A source shows the 1150-era water-gate examples required stationary harbor cranes.
The port_crane_harbor_works node is redated and rescoped to earlier generic water-gate infrastructure rather than dockside cranes.
short_reason: Fortified water gates are defensive wall access points, not downstream products of medieval port cranes.
